note: A generator inlines the compact_prompt fragments of the selected patterns into its prompt. It MUST NOT depend on fetching this file at run time; links are optional enrichment. Patterns with converter_ready=false are held back from prompt packs until the converter supports them. Deck-subtype selections (which patterns a goal reaches for) live in consulting/packs/*.json.

support_legend.partial: converts, but a dedicated pattern/converter pass would improve it
support_legend.gap: needs converter work before it converts well

families.label: Consulting / strategy
families.family_rule: Action titles: each slide title is a full-sentence finding, and the titles read top-to-bottom as the whole argument. One message per slide. Answer first (pyramid), then MECE support, then next steps. Evidence sits beside its claim; every data slide carries a 'Source:' line. Colour directs attention, it does not decorate.

archetypes.name: Boardroom Classic
archetypes.desc: White, restrained, dense evidence — the safe default.
archetypes.best_for: recommendations, value cases, market sizing, M&A logic
archetypes.visual_language: white, restrained, dense evidence, footnotes, blue/grey accent
archetypes.rule: white background, restrained and dense; footnotes and source lines; evidence packed but calm — let the numbers carry it.

archetypes.name: Modern Insight
archetypes.hint: Open whitespace, one idea per slide
archetypes.desc: Lots of whitespace and simple charts — one idea per slide.
archetypes.best_for: research readouts, thought leadership, executive insight
archetypes.visual_language: open whitespace, simple charts, one confident accent, few boxes, small multiples
archetypes.rule: generous whitespace, one confident accent, simple charts and small multiples, very few boxes — make one idea breathe per slide.

archetypes.name: Operator Redline
archetypes.hint: Strong top rule, blunt and commercial
archetypes.desc: Punchy and commercial — a strong top rule, accent used sparingly.
archetypes.best_for: commercial strategy, market studies, pragmatic recommendations
archetypes.visual_language: strong top rule, red accent used sparingly, direct ranked charts, source/confidentiality footer
archetypes.rule: a strong top rule, the accent used sparingly for emphasis, direct ranked charts, a source/confidentiality line in the footer — blunt and commercial.

archetypes.name: Research Bureau
archetypes.desc: Reads like an institute report — chapter dividers and big numerals.
archetypes.best_for: public research, survey decks, institute reports
archetypes.visual_language: chapter navigation, full-bleed section dividers with a large numeral, methodology, report flow
archetypes.rule: chapter navigation, full-bleed section dividers with a large numeral, a methodology note, report-style flow — reads like an institute report.

archetypes.name: Systems Blueprint
archetypes.desc: Boxes, swimlanes and diagrams — structure over prose.
archetypes.visual_language: modular boxes, swimlanes, capability maps, more tables and diagrams, strong section labels
archetypes.rule: modular boxes, swimlanes and capability maps, more tables and diagrams, strong section labels — structure over prose.

archetypes.name: Executive Brief
archetypes.desc: A dense but readable board memo — strict hierarchy, summary up front.
archetypes.best_for: leadership updates, macro briefings, board memos
archetypes.visual_language: blue/neutral accent, dense-but-readable memo slides, executive summaries, section dividers, strict hierarchy
archetypes.rule: a neutral, dense-but-readable memo; an executive summary up front; strict hierarchy and section dividers — board-memo discipline.

patterns.name: Cover / document title
patterns.compact_prompt: Cover: the document title slide — a short topic title (one accent word via <em>), a one-line subtitle, and client/date/confidentiality in the corners. Set the scene; no body content.

patterns.name: Executive summary
patterns.compact_prompt: Executive summary: open with the recommendation in one bold line, then 3-5 supporting findings as a numbered list or a stat row. State the answer first; the rest of the deck defends it.

patterns.name: Agenda / storyline
patterns.compact_prompt: Agenda / storyline: preview the argument as 3-6 numbered sections with action-oriented names (a claim each, not a label) — reading them should sketch the whole story. A simple numbered list.

patterns.name: Section divider
patterns.compact_prompt: Section divider: a full-slide chapter break — a large section number plus a short section title on a clean field. Marks a shift; carries no evidence.

patterns.name: Action-title evidence slide
patterns.job: Put one claim and its proof together
patterns.compact_prompt: Action-title evidence slide: the title states the finding as a full sentence; use data-unpaper-lay='2l' with a short so-what (2-3 bullets) on the left and ONE proof object (chart or table) on the right; put a 'Source:' line directly under the evidence.

patterns.name: Single chart proof
patterns.compact_prompt: Single chart proof: one data-unpaper-chart carries the whole point; the title states what the chart proves; one source line below; no second visual.

patterns.name: Small multiples
patterns.job: Compare several charts on one scale
patterns.compact_prompt: Small multiples: 2-4 charts of the SAME type and identical scale side by side (data-unpaper-lay='2' or '3') to compare segments or periods; one shared source line.

patterns.name: Waterfall / bridge
patterns.job: Explain change from baseline to outcome
patterns.compact_prompt: Waterfall/bridge (.wf block): walk a total from start to end through +/- steps. Position each bar by its running total in baked px (baseline at the plot bottom, scale ~2.5px/unit; .up green / .down red / .total dark); join steps with thin .wf-conn lines at the pre-step total; label each delta (+/-) and the start/end totals. Copy the Value-bridge slide's geometry and keep the plot within the slide so it converts to native shapes.

patterns.name: Variance decomposition
patterns.compact_prompt: Variance decomposition: break a total change into its drivers — a waterfall (.wf) or a chart plus a .stats row naming each driver contribution. Show what moved the number and by how much.

patterns.name: KPI stat row
patterns.compact_prompt: KPI stat row (.stats): anchor the story in 2-4 big numbers, each with a tiny label and optional delta. One row of native text — the headline metrics, not a table.

patterns.name: Benchmark ranking
patterns.compact_prompt: Benchmark ranking: rank companies / countries / segments / options on one metric — a sorted horizontal bar chart (data-unpaper-chart) or a ranked table, highest to lowest, with the leader called out. Keep labels short.

patterns.name: Market map / 2x2
patterns.compact_prompt: 2x2 matrix: position options on two named axes using the matrix block (.matrix.axes) with four quadrants, clear axis labels, and the priority cell marked; plot 4-8 items max.

patterns.name: Issue tree
patterns.job: Break a problem into MECE branches
patterns.compact_prompt: Issue tree: break the question into MECE branches (a parent box over 2-4 child boxes, each splitting further if needed) using the org/tree block; branches must be mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive.

patterns.name: Option comparison
patterns.compact_prompt: Option comparison: a real <table> with options as rows and criteria as columns (<=6 columns); mark the recommended row or column; one source line below.

patterns.name: Scorecard / Harvey balls
patterns.compact_prompt: Scorecard: a scorecard grid of options x criteria; each cell a Harvey-ball rating (data-unpaper-rating 0-1, drawn as a filled-pie SVG: empty, quarter, half, three-quarter, full) or a RAG colour; add a one-line legend and a source line. Use the .scorecard grid, not a native <table>, so each ball stays a movable shape.

patterns.name: Maturity ladder
patterns.job: Show levels from current to target state
patterns.compact_prompt: Maturity ladder: levels from current to target state — 3-5 stacked rungs (cards or a vertical .timeline), each a level name plus a one-line descriptor, with today level marked. Show the climb.

patterns.name: Roadmap / phases
patterns.compact_prompt: Roadmap: 3-5 phases over time using the timeline block, each with a date/label and 1-2 outcomes, laid out left to right.

patterns.name: Process flow
patterns.compact_prompt: Process flow: 3-5 ordered steps joined by arrows using the flow block; one short label per step; arrows in the accent colour.

patterns.name: Operating model
patterns.compact_prompt: Operating model: capabilities, roles and interfaces — a grid of capability boxes (cards) grouped into layers, or an .orgtree of functions. Show the structure, not a process.

patterns.name: Org / governance
patterns.compact_prompt: Org / governance (.orgtree): show accountability and escalation — a small hierarchy of boxes (sponsor to lead to workstreams). Native boxes plus connectors; keep to 2-3 levels.

patterns.name: Initiative portfolio
patterns.job: Sort initiatives by impact, effort, status
patterns.compact_prompt: Initiative portfolio: sort ~6-8 initiatives by impact x effort on a 2x2, or as status-tagged cards; mark the quick wins.

patterns.name: Business case / value sizing
patterns.job: Explain value pool and financial impact
patterns.compact_prompt: Business case / value sizing: size the value and its financials — a waterfall (.wf) bridging from baseline to the value pool, plus an assumptions table. State the number and what it rests on.

patterns.name: Risk and mitigation
patterns.job: Pair risks with controls and actions
patterns.compact_prompt: Risk and mitigation: pair each risk with its control — a table (Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation | Owner) or paired cards. One row per risk; rate likelihood/impact with words or Harvey balls.

patterns.name: Stakeholder map
patterns.compact_prompt: Stakeholder map: place stakeholders on influence x interest — a 2x2 (data-unpaper-lay=quad / .matrix.axes) with named dots per quadrant and a one-line engagement note per zone.

patterns.name: Segment / persona comparison
patterns.compact_prompt: Segment / persona comparison: compare 2-4 customer or market segments across the same few attributes — a table with one column per segment (or cards), so differences read across a row.

patterns.name: Case example
patterns.job: Show one proof point or vignette
patterns.compact_prompt: Case example: one proof point or vignette — a short narrative beside a pulled-out result, via data-unpaper-lay=2l or a .callout. Make the abstract concrete with a single real case.

patterns.name: Recommendation / decision
patterns.job: Close with the ask, decision, next step
patterns.compact_prompt: Recommendation close: the decision/ask in one bold line, the 2-3 reasons, and the first concrete next step with an owner and a date.

patterns.name: Appendix data table
patterns.compact_prompt: Appendix data table: preserve the detailed evidence — a compact, dense table with many rows, small type and clear headers. For backup; not a headline slide.

patterns.name: Methodology / sources
patterns.compact_prompt: Methodology / sources: explain the research basis — a short list of method steps and the data sources used, as bullets or a .callout. Builds trust; keep it brief.

patterns.name: Assumptions / limitations
patterns.compact_prompt: Assumptions / limitations: state the model boundaries — a short bulleted list or .callout of the key assumptions and what the analysis does not cover. Be explicit and honest.

patterns.name: Glossary / definitions
patterns.compact_prompt: Glossary / definitions: define the terms — a two-column list of term plus short definition (a table or definition list). Alphabetical; one line each.

patterns.name: Before / after
patterns.compact_prompt: Before / after: current state vs target state side by side — data-unpaper-lay=2 with Today on the left and Target on the right, the same dimensions in each, so the gap is obvious.

patterns.name: Recommendation tree
patterns.compact_prompt: Recommendation tree: link the recommendation to its actions — an .orgtree (or rows) from the headline recommendation down to 2-4 supporting actions/owners. Show how the ask decomposes into moves.
